the skip rate.

next!', then that counts
against the song in the algorithm.

You have met Lady Gaga and you asked
her how long a pop song should be.

How did she take it?
She fixed me with this steely gaze.
Hey, it's Hannah here.

Welcome back to What in the World
from the BBC World Service.

Quick question for you. How long
do you think a pop song should be?

For years they've been getting shorter.
Some are just two or three minutes long,

and a lot of people say
that's because of the TikTok effect,

because you just need one catchy hook
to go viral.

But now song times are beginning
to creep back up.

Some are even seven or nine minutes
long. So what is going on here?

And are longer songs just as popular
as short ones?

Well, I'm here with the best person
in the business to talk about it.

The BBC Music correspondent,
Mark Savage. Hi. Welcome.

Hello, Hannah.
How are you doing?

Very well. So I read
that you have met Lady Gaga,

and you put
that very same question to her.

You asked her how long a pop song
should be. How did she take it?

She fixed me with this steely gaze,
and it was a kind of withering look

as she said, 'the perfect length
for a pop song is how ever long

the artist wants it to be',
which is a very Lady Gaga answer.

I was delighted. Nothing better
than getting a reaction like that

from a pop star when you're doing an
interview and you know she's right.

It should be up to the artist
how long her song is.

But that's not the story of the music
industry over the last ten years.

Technology always drives change
in music, going right back

to the days of people listening
on cassette and seven inch singles.

You know the - on vinyl, the longest
a song could be was three minutes,

because that was how much space you had
before it compromised the sound quality.

And then more recently, with
streaming, there's been a pressure

to make songs shorter because Spotify
has something called the skip rate.

So if you are listening to a song and
you just go, 'I'm fed up with this,

next!', then that counts
against the song in the algorithm.

And so it gets recommended
to fewer people.

And the number of streams
that a song gets goes down.

And that means you, the artist
or the writer, gets less money.

So there's an imperative
to make the song short

so that people stick around right
to the end and get a boost.

I didn't know that. I didn't know
that when I skipped ahead,

I was actually having a negative
effect on a song's algorithm.

So apart from people skipping
through songs

and artists really wanting listeners
to get to the end of their tracks,

why else have
songs been getting shorter?

So I think
TikTok has a lot to answer for here.

You know that your attention span
when you're flicking

through the 'For You' feed
on your apps is very, very short.

If something doesn't grab your
attention in the first three seconds,
you just swipe up.

And that has really driven songs
to become instantly catchy.

You don't have twenty seconds to set the
scene and do a nice long introduction.
The hook has got to come first.

And actually one
of the things that we've seen

is that people have moved the chorus
of their song right to the front.

It used to be you would have an intro
and then a verse,

and then maybe a bridge, and
the chorus would be the culmination.

You know, something you build up to,
now you're just straight in there

because you've got to get
people's attention straight away.

And when the chorus comes first,
well, then you can kind of dispense
with some of the other bits as well.
And so we've seen songs shrink.
You know, in the 1990s,

the average length of a hit song
in the UK was above four minutes.

In 2019, the year that TikTok
achieved mainstream popularity,

it dropped to three minutes
and twelve seconds,
which is the lowest it has ever been.

Now we're seeing a bit of a change,
a bit of a pushback against that.

But songs are still, even now, a minute
shorter than they were thirty years ago.

And how short are some
of the shortest ones? The big ones?
The big ones.

So PinkPantheress is probably
the poster child for short songs.

You know, all of her songs
are basically two minutes long.

She has one called 'Attracted to You',
that's just sixty-seven seconds,

but it's got half a billion streams
on Spotify and Lil Nas X Old Town Road,
that was one minute and fifty-eight seconds.

You know, songs really have been
compressed and sometimes that's good.

You know, sometimes it's hard
to write an extra verse
and make it as good as the first one.

And it's hard to write a middle eight
or to take a song somewhere else.

Some songs work better
in that format,

but I think also, if you're an artist
who has something to say,

if you've got a message in your song,
if you've got something really deep

and personal that you want to convey,
then you need that extra space

to let the words breathe, to let
people feel what you're going through.

So you need to give it
that breathing room.

It's really interesting
that TikTok has just changed the way

that people structure songs,
as well as the length of songs.

And I was reading, it was changing
the way that artists write them as well,

because sometimes they were just writing
the hooks, testing them out on socials

and then seeing if it hits and if it
does, then writing the rest of the song.

So Lil Nas X and PinkPantheress,
the two artists

that I mentioned, I mean, that's
basically their business model.

They use TikTok
as a kind of echo chamber.

You know, if a song picks up
enough likes, then they'll finish it.

But if the idea that they post just
kind of withers and dies,

well, there's no point in pursuing it
because people might not be interested.

And that's a really interesting thing.
It's focus grouping, right? Right.

And that's never happened before
in the music industry.

You know, you would have used to go
into a studio for a month,

work as hard as you can to perfect
the song to make it sound right,

and then it goes out into the world
and you just wait to see what happens.

But now you can get instant feedback
as you're writing.

And in some cases,
I guess that's great,

but I suppose it also makes
you subject to writing by committee,

doing what people want to hear,
rather than what means something to you.

So it's a tricky balance
for artists to pull off.

Do you think, overall, that shorter
songs are making artists more money?

This is the thing. So
if we go back to the Spotify model,

if you have a nine minute song
and somebody plays it once,

you will get roughly a third of
$0.01 in Spotify royalties.

Now, if you've got a song
that's a minute and a half long

and people listen to that for
nine minutes, well, that counts as

six plays and you will get six times
$0.03 of a cent.

So it makes more business sense
to write a shorter song

because you get paid
more frequently, because

all that counts is the number
of times that a song has been played.

I'm going to pause you here because

I have noticed a slight change in the

Sometimes I make songs

I'll only share

I do think there are a lot of downsides,

namely when it comes
to your own mental health.

I have had several moments

And then months in between,
where you're in a valley

and whatever you're doing,

And I think
those waves of extreme highs

and extreme lows can really, really
be detrimental to your mental health.

So you really have to know, one,

And two, knowing when to take breaks

I personally don't think it's changed

because since the beginning I've

However, I will say
because TikTok in 2020

and TikTok in 2022 isn't the same

And I think the real challenge
comes down to finding ways to make it

as engaging and as captivating

So maybe the way I promote it

but I don't think the way
I've created music

has fundamentally changed

Why do you think that is?

Well, the analysis that we've done,

every year for the

that low point

That's in 2019 and 2020.

This year,
in the first six months of 2025,

that's gone back
up to three minutes twenty-four.

So a twelve second increase.
That sounds quite small,

I love that.

Yeah, that's almost five minutes long.

We've just had

That was a huge TikTok viral hit.

about being messed around by

'Pink Pony Club' is a song

I've seen people

That parental relationship

Exactly. Yeah.

And I think the artists that
